coredata.dc:description: The COVID-19 pandemic remains an ongoing global health threat, yet effective treatments are still lacking. This has led to a high demand for complementary/alternative medicine, such as Chinese herbal medicines for curbing the COVID-19 pandemic. Given the dual anticancer and antiviral activities of many herbal drugs, they may hold a multifaceted potential to tackle both cancer and SARS-CoV-2. Triptolide is the major bioactive compound isolated from Tripterygium wilfordii Hook F (TwHF), a traditional Chinese medicinal herb recognized for its beneficial pharmacological properties in many diseases, including cancer and viral infection. However, its application in the clinic has been greatly limited due to its toxicity and poor water solubility. Here, from a screen of a natural compound library of Chinese Pharmacopoeia, we identified triptolide as a top candidate to inhibit cell entry of SARS-CoV-2. We demonstrated that triptolide robustly blocked viral entry at nanomolar concentrations in cellular models, with broad range activity against emerging Omicron variants of SARS-CoV-2. Mechanistically, triptolide disrupted the interaction of SARS-CoV-2 spike protein with its receptor ACE2. Furthermore, we synthesized water-soluble, triptolide-derived carbon quantum dots. Compared to triptolide, these highly biocompatible nanomaterials exhibited prominent antiviral capabilities against Omicron variants of SARS-CoV-2 with less cytotoxicity. Finally, we showed that triptolide-derived carbonized materials excelled in their anticancer properties compared to triptolide and Minnelide, a water-soluble analog of triptolide. Together, our results provide a rationale for the potential development of triptolide-carbonized derivatives as a promising antiviral candidate for the current pandemic and future outbreaks, as well as anticancer agents.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Screen of natural compounds from the Chinese Pharmacopoeia. (A) A schematic of the screening strategy. 293T-ACE2 cells were treated with 225 compounds at a final concentration of 10 μM. Next, the cells were infected with SARS-CoV2 wild-type spike Vpp with a MOI of 0.1. The relative luciferase activity was detected 24 h post-pseudovirus inoculation. (B) The Z-score for the inhibition of Vpp infection of 225 compounds. The inhibition of Vpp infection was determined by the relative luciferase activity normalized with DMSO. Highlighted in red is triptolide. (C) Inhibition of Vpp infection and cytotoxicity in 293T-ACE2 cells. Cells were treated with different doses of triptolide, followed by infection with wild-type spike Vpp at an MOI of 0.25 for 293T-ACE2 cells for 24 h. The y-axis represents relative infection rate calculated by the relative luciferase activity normalized with DMSO. The relative luciferase activity was measured by BrightGlo Luminescent Assay. As for the cytotoxicity, cells were treated with different doses of triptolide for 24 h. The y-axis represents relative cell viability measured by CellTiterGlo Luminescent Assay and normalized with DMSO. (D) Inhibition of Vpp infection and cytotoxicity in H1650 cells. (E) Inhibition of Vpp infection and cytotoxicity in Calu-3 cells. (F) Inhibition of Vpp infection and cytotoxicity in Vero E6 cels. (G) The EC50 and CC50 of triptolide were determined by using GraphPad PRISM software. The selectivity index (SI) was calculated by CC50/EC50 ratio. All data are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3).

Triptolide blocked Vpp entry of SARS-CoV-2 VOCs and Omicron variants. (A) Inhibition of Vpp infection of SARS-CoV-2 VOCs in 293T-ACE2 cells. Cells were treated with different doses of triptolide, followed by infection with various Vpp at an MOI of 0.25 for 24 h. The relative infection rate was calculated by the relative luciferase activity and normalized with DMSO. (B) Inhibition of Vpp infection of SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variants in 293T-ACE2 cells. Cells were treated with different doses of triptolide, followed by infection with various Vpp at an MOI of 0.25 for 24 h. The relative infection rate was calculated by the relative luciferase activity normalized with DMSO. (C) Inhibition of Vpp infection of SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variants in H1650 cells. Cells were treated with different doses of triptolide, followed by infection with various Vpp at an MOI of 1 for 24 h. The relative infection rate was calculated by the relative luciferase activity normalized with DMSO. All data are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3).

Characterization of Triptolide-CQDs. (A) Schematic representation of one-step heating synthesis of Triptolide-CQDs. (B) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Triptolide-CQDs. Inserts: high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images. (C) X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of triptolide, mannose, and Triptolide-CQDs. (D) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS spectra of Triptolide-CQDs. (E) UV–Vis absorption of triptolide, mannose, and Triptolide-CQDs. (F) Fluorescence spectra of triptolide, mannose, and Triptolide-CQDs. (G) Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of triptolide, mannose, and Triptolide-CQDs.

Triptolide-CQDs exhibited superior antiviral activity against SARS-CoV-2 pseudovirus. (A) Mannose-CNPs (carbonized nanomaterials prepared solely from mannose at 120 °C) did not affect Vpp entry of SARS-CoV-2.293T-ACE2 cells were treated with different doses of Mannose-CNPs, followed by infection with wild-type spike Vpp at an MOI of 0.25 for 24 h. The relative infection rate was calculated by the relative luciferase activity normalized with DMSO. (B) Relative infection rate of Triptolide-CNPs (carbonized nanomaterials prepared solely from triptolide at 120 °C). 293T-ACE2 cells were treated with different doses of Triptolide-CNPs, followed by infection with wild-type spike Vpp at an MOI of 0.25 for 24 h. The relative infection rate was calculated by the relative luciferase activity normalized with DMSO. (C) Inhibition of Vpp infection in 293T-ACE2 cells by Triptolide-CQDs-120, Triptolide-CQDs-150, and Triptolide-CQDs-180. Cells were treated with different doses of Triptolide-CQDs, followed by infection with wild-type spike Vpp at an MOI of 0.25 for 24 h. The relative infection rate was calculated by the relative luciferase activity normalized with DMSO. (D) Cytotoxicity of Mannose-CNPs. Cells were treated with different doses of Mannose-CNPs for 24 h. The y-axis represents relative cell viability measured by CellTiterGlo Luminescent Assay and normalized with DMSO. (E) Cytotoxicity of Triptolide-CQDs. Cells were treated with different doses of Triptolide-CQDs for 24 h. The y-axis represents relative cell viability measured by CellTiterGlo Luminescent Assay and normalized with DMSO. (F) The EC50 and CC50 of Triptolide-CQDs were determined by using GraphPad PRISM software. The selectivity index (SI) was calculated by CC50/EC50 ratio. (G) Comparison of triptolide, Minnelide, and Triptolide-CQDs-120 in the inhibition of Vpp infection of SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant B.1.1.529. Cells were treated with different doses of triptolide, Minnelide or Triptolide-CQDs-120, followed by infection with B.1.1.52 Vpp at an MOI of 0.25 for 24 h. The relative infection rate was calculated by the relative luciferase activity normalized with DMSO. For cytotoxicity assay, cells were treated with different doses of triptolide, Minnelide or Triptolide-CQDs-120 for 24 h. The relative cell viability was measured by CellTiterGlo Luminescent Assay and normalized with DMSO. (H) Comparison of triptolide, Minnelide and Triptolide-CQDs-120 in the inhibition of Vpp infection in H1650 cells. All the experiments were done in 3 biological replicates. All data are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3). P < 0.05 (∗).

Triptolide interrupted spike-ACE2 interaction. (A) Different doses of triptolide were incubated with biotinylated spike S1, Eu-labelled ACE2 and dye-labelled acceptor for 1 h, followed by measurement of TR-FRET signal by a fluorescence reader. The experiments were done in triplicates. Binding of triptolide (B) and Triptolide-CQDs-120 (C) with wild-type spike protein and human ACE2 protein measured by SPR assay. Affinities were measured using Biacore T200 analysis with a CM5 sensor chip. Sensors coated with recombinant spike protein or ACE2 were exposed to various concentrations of triptolide (TPL) or Triptolide-CQDs-120 (CQD) (0.195, 0.391, 0.781, 1.563, 3.125, 6.25, 12.5, 25, and 50 μM) for association, followed by dissociation in an analyte-free solution. Binding affinities were analyzed using Biacore T200 Evaluation Software with a 1:1 binding model. The equilibrium dissociation constant (Kd) for TPL binding to recombinant ACE2 and spike was 4.33 μM and 135 μM, respectively. For CQD, the Kd values were 0.88 μM and 0.91 μM for ACE2 and spike, respectively. RU, resonance units. (D) Replicon assay. 293T cells were transfected with SARS-CoV-2 replicon, followed by treatment with different doses of Triptolide-CQDs-120. The relative luciferase activity was measured 48 h after drug treatment by Dual-Glo Luciferase Assay System. Remdesivir (RDV) was used as the positive control. The relative cell viability was measured by CellTiterGlo Luminescent Assay and normalized with DMSO. All the experiments were done in 3 biological replicates. All data are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3). P < 0.05 (∗).

ARID1A deficiency predicts sensitivity to triptolide in ovarian cancer cells. (A) Correlations of ovarian cancer cell proliferation fold change under drugs (from PRISM v2 database) vs ARID1A downregulation (from DepMap). (B) Correlation between triptolide survival vs ARID1A fitness in human ovarian cancer cells. (C) Generation of murine ovarian tumor cell line ID8-derived Arid1a KO cells. Western blot of ARID1A and β-tubulin in ID8-control and two KO clones. (D) Cell viability assay of ID8-control and Arid1a KO cells treated with different doses of triptolide for 4 days. (E) ID8-control and Arid1a KO cells were treated with triptolide at the indicated concentrations for 6 days, and the cells were fixed for colony formation assay. The number of colonies was normalized to that in the vehicle control group. Cell viability assay of ID8-control (F) and Arid1a KO-1 (G) cells treated with different concentrations of triptolide for 3 days. The medium was washed out and replaced with fresh medium without triptolide at 12, 18, 24 and 30 h post-treatment. The relative cell survival was normalized to that in the vehicle control group. All the experiments were done in 3 biological replicates. All data are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3). P < 0.05 (∗).

Triptolide reduced MYC levels at both mRNA and posttranslational levels. (A) Lysates from ID8-cotrol cells or Arid1a-KO-1 cells treated with 50 nM triptolide for different time points were immunoblotted for MYC and GAPDH. (B) Relative MYC protein levels from 3 independent experiments of (A) that were normalized to vehicle control. Data were analyzed using unpaired, 1-tailed Student's t-test. ∗P < 0.05. (C) MYC mRNA expression in ID8-cotrol cells or Arid1a-KO-1 cells treated with 50 nM triptolide for different time points was measured by RT-qPCR (n = 3) and analyzed by 1-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett's post hoc test. ∗∗P < 0.01. (D) ID8-control or Arid1a-KO-1 cells were treated with 50 nM triptolide for 2 h before treatment with 25 μM CHX. Data were analyzed using unpaired, 1-tailed Student's t-test. ∗∗P < 0.01.

Triptolide-CQDs excelled in the anticancer properties against ARID1A-deficient ovarian cancer cells. (A) ID8-control cells were treated with different compounds at the indicated concentrations for 6 days, and the cells were fixed for colony formation assay. (B) The number of colonies was normalized to that in the vehicle control group. (C) ID8-control cells were treated with different compounds at the indicated concentrations for 4 days. The relative cell viability was measured by CellTiterGlo Luminescent Assay and normalized with the vehicle control group. (D) Arid1a-KO cells were treated with different compounds at the indicated concentrations for 6 days, and the cells were fixed for colony formation assay. (E) The number of colonies was normalized to that in the vehicle control group for ID8-control and Arid1a-KO cells. All the experiments were done in 3 biological replicates. All data are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3). P < 0.05 (∗).

Triptolide-CQDs attenuated tumor growth in an orthotopic syngeneic ovarian cancer model. (A) Schematic representation of the orthotopic syngeneic ovarian cancer model. Luciferase-expressing ID8-Arid1a KO-1 cells were implanted into female C57BL/6 mice through intrabursal injection 7 days before starting vehicle control (saline) or Triptolide-CQDs-180 (0.8 mg/kg, intraperitoneally, daily) dosing. Tumor growth was monitored by using IVIS imaging system every week. Tumor weight was measured every day to monitor toxicity. (B) IVIS images of mice treated with control or Triptolide-CQDs-180 (n = 5). (C) Tumor growth of mice treated with control or Triptolide-CQDs-180 (n = 5). Statistical significance was assessed using an unpaired, 1-tailed Student's t-test. ∗∗P < 0.01. (D) Body weight of mice treated with control or Triptolide-CQDs-180 (n = 5).
