ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The COVID-19 pandemic remains an ongoing global health threat, yet effective treatments are still lacking. This has led to a high demand for complementary/alternative medicine, such as Chinese herbal medicines for curbing the COVID-19 pandemic. Given the dual anticancer and antiviral activities of many herbal drugs, they may hold a multifaceted potential to tackle both cancer and SARS-CoV-2. Triptolide is the major bioactive compound isolated from Tripterygium wilfordii Hook F (TwHF), a traditional Chinese medicinal herb recognized for its beneficial pharmacological properties in many diseases, including cancer and viral infection. However, its application in the clinic has been greatly limited due to its toxicity and poor water solubility. Here, from a screen of a natural compound library of Chinese Pharmacopoeia, we identified triptolide as a top candidate to inhibit cell entry of SARS-CoV-2. We demonstrated that triptolide robustly blocked viral entry at nanomolar concentrations in cellular models, with broad range activity against emerging Omicron variants of SARS-CoV-2. Mechanistically, triptolide disrupted the interaction of SARS-CoV-2 spike protein with its receptor ACE2. Furthermore, we synthesized water-soluble, triptolide-derived carbon quantum dots. Compared to triptolide, these highly biocompatible nanomaterials exhibited prominent antiviral capabilities against Omicron variants of SARS-CoV-2 with less cytotoxicity. Finally, we showed that triptolide-derived carbonized materials excelled in their anticancer properties compared to triptolide and Minnelide, a water-soluble analog of triptolide. Together, our results provide a rationale for the potential development of triptolide-carbonized derivatives as a promising antiviral candidate for the current pandemic and future outbreaks, as well as anticancer agents.

ce:section.ce:para: Triptolide was first isolated in 1972 from a vine plant, Tripterygium wilfordii Hook F (TwHF, also known as Thunder God Vine), a Chinese medicinal herb that has been used as a remedy for many diseases for centuries [1]. Among 400 natural ingredients isolated and characterized from TwHF, triptolide possesses the most potent anti-proliferative properties, which has attracted considerable investigations on its antitumor effects in a variety of cancers, as well as the preclinical and clinical assessments of triptolide and its analogs as anticancer agents [2]. Despite significant beneficial pharmacological properties of triptolide in cancers and other diseases, its application in the clinic has been greatly limited due to its toxicity and poor water solubility. As such, numerous analogs of triptolide have been recently developed and novel drug delivery methods have been investigated to enhance its efficacy while limiting its toxicity [3]. Minnelide, a water-soluble analog of triptolide, has demonstrated potent anticancer properties in various preclinical cancer models and is currently under clinical evaluation for treating advanced solid tumors and leukemia [2,4]. A recent phase I study (NCT05566834) on the evaluation of maximum tolerated dose, safety, and antitumor activity of Minnelide in patients with advanced gastric cancer showed meaningful clinical efficacy with a manageable safety profile [5].

In addition to antitumor properties, given the potent anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive functions of triptolide, triptolide has been reported to inhibit virus infection, such as HIV-1 [6], HSV-1 [7,8], and influenza virus [9]. For example, there are ongoing clinical trials for HIV-1 infected patients to assess the impact of triptolide on virus reservoir and T cell immune activation and inflammation (NCT02115659, NCT03403569, NCT02002286 and NCT02219672). A recently completed clinical trial (NCT04084444) showed that (5R)-5-hydroxytriptolide (LLDT-8), a triptolide derivative with a lower cytotoxicity, alleviated inflammation and enhanced T cell recovery [10]. However, the antiviral spectrum of triptolide is not fully defined and its underlying antiviral mechanisms incompletely understood.

Chinese herbal medicine has been widely used to treat epidemic diseases such as severe acute respiratory syndrome (SARS) infection in 2002, and has been proposed for treatment for coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) caused by severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) [11]. As of September 2024, SARS-CoV-2 has caused more than 776 million confirmed cases and more than 7 million deaths globally [12]. Since the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic, multiple SARS-CoV-2 variants, including Alpha (B.1.1.7), Beta (B.1.351), Gamma (B.1.1.28.1), Delta (B.1.617.2), and Omicron (B.1.1.529) strains have been declared as the major Variants of Concern (VOCs) by the World Health Organization (WHO). The Omicron variants, first identified in November 2021, have quickly surged to global dominance. Several sublineages of Omicron variants have appeared and overtaken each other, with BA.2.86 and JN.1 being currently circulating Variants of Interest (VOIs) as of September 2024 [12]. Despite the availability of vaccines as the most promising approach for curbing the COVID-19 pandemic, the emerging variants pose a threat to the efficacy of current vaccines and therapeutic antibodies [13]. Therefore, additional effective treatments are urgently needed to limit morbidity and mortality of COVID-19. Indeed, numerous Chinese herbal medicines have been shown to improve the clinical outcome of COVID-19 as an adjunct therapy [14–20]. Importantly, many components in herbal plants and dietary food have been shown to possess anti-SARS-CoV-2 activity, such as peimine from Fritillaria [21], Dauricine from Menispermum dauricum [22], herbal formula of Kang Guan Recipe [23], tannic acids in teas [24–26], and isochlorogenic acid of coffee ingredients [27,28].

Here, we conducted an unbiased screen from a library containing 225 natural compounds in Chinese Pharmacopoeia. We identified triptolide as a top candidate to inhibit SARS-CoV-2 cell entry. By using the viral pseudo-particles, we demonstrated that triptolide effectively blocked viral entry at nanomolar concentrations in cellular models, with broad range activity against emerging Omicron variants of SARS-CoV-2. Furthermore, the molecular biology assay indicated that triptolide disrupted the interaction of viral spike protein with its receptor ACE2. To enhance therapeutic efficacy and reduce potential cytotoxicity, we further prepared water-soluble triptolide-derived carbon quantum dots (Triptolide-CQDs) that possessed prominent antiviral capabilities against the Omicron variants of SARS-CoV-2, but exhibited a lower cytotoxicity as compared with triptolide.

Finally, utilizing bioinformatic datasets that integrate cellular drug response with cellular survival upon downregulation of AT-rich interactive domain 1 A (ARID1A), one of the most frequently mutated genes across human cancers [29], we found that ARID1A deficiency predicts sensitivity to triptolide in ovarian cancer cells. We subsequently validated this finding to show that triptolide-derived carbonized materials excelled in their anticancer properties compared to triptolide and Minnelide. Together, our results suggest that the highly biocompatible Triptolide-CQDs may be developed as a promising antiviral candidate against SARS-CoV-2 infection, and potentially anticancer agents as well.

Human embryonic kidney cells 293T stably expressing recombinant human ACE2 (293T-ACE2) were generated by transfection with the pLAS2w.ACE2.Pbsd (provided by RNA Technology Platform and Gene Manipulation Core, Academia Sinica, Taiwan), followed by selection in 20 μg/mL of blasticidin. 293T-ACE2 cells and African green monkey kidney epithelial cell line Vero E6 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Human lung cancer cell lines H1650 and Calu-3 cells were cultured in Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 Medium (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Both media were supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, Hyclone, USA) and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The mouse ovarian cancer cell line ID8, obtained from the laboratory of Prof. Iain A. McNeish (Institute of Cancer Sciences, University of Glasgow, Scotland) [30,31] were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 4 % FBS, 1 % penicillin/streptomycin, and ITS (5 μg/mL insulin, 5 μg/mL transferrin, and 5 ng/mL sodium selenite). All cell lines were negative for Mycoplasma contamination. All cells were maintained at 37 °C under 5 % CO2.

To generate Arid1a knockout (KO) ID8 cells, Arid1a_gRNA #1 (GTGATATCCCGCCGAATCAT) and Arid1a_gRNA #2 (GCGGGCTCACGTAGACAGGA) were cloned into pX330-GFP-U6 plasmid (provided by Dr. Chang-Mei Liu, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing), which was modified from pX330 (Addgene #42230). Plasmid transfections were performed by using Lipofectamine 2000 following the manufacturer's instructions (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Cells were incubated for 72 h, and GFP-positive single cells were sorted by flow cytometry, followed by clonal expansion and verification by Western blot.

The natural compound library in Chinese Pharmacopoeia (#L6800) was purchased from TargetMol, USA. Triptolide (#645900) and Minnelide (#HY-124584) were purchased from Sigma, USA and MedChemExpress, USA, respectively. Cycloheximide (CHX, #C7698) was purchased from Sigma, USA.

Gene fitness data was calculated based on CRISPR and RNAi data from DepMap 24Q2 dataset [32] as described before [33]. Cancer cell line sensitivity to triptolide was downloaded from PRISM2 drug screen [34]. Spearman correlation between gene fitness data and response to 6658 drugs was correlated using data for 35 ovarian cancer cell lines. Correlations with p < 0.001 were considered statistically significant.

Triptolide-CQDs were synthesized essentially as previously described [35,36] by using a heating method. Briefly, a mixture of triptolide and mannose powder (1:1) was heated in a muffle furnace at 120, 150, or 180 °C for 2 h to yield orange or brown residues. These residues were cooled to room temperature and dissolved in sodium phosphate buffer, followed by sonication and subsequent centrifugation to remove large particles. The brownish-yellow supernatant was then dialyzed in sodium chloride solution through a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 0.5–1 kDa, Float-A-Lyzer G2, Spectrum Laboratories, Rancho Dominguez, USA) for 5 h. Finally, the sodium chloride solution was replaced with 50 % ethanol or ultrapure water and dialyzed for 18 h. The purified Triptolide-CQD solution was stored at 4 °C when not in use. The concentration of purified Triptolide-CQDs in the solution (in terms of mg/mL) was determined by the freeze-drying method. Further characterization of Triptolide-CQDs was performed essentially as previously described [35,36]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Triptolide-CQDs were acquired using a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN system (Philips/FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA) operating at 200 kV. For sample preparation, Triptolide-CQDs (0.1 mg/mL) were deposited onto 300-mesh carbon-coated copper grids, and the excess solvent was allowed to evaporate at ambient temperature and pressure. Absorption measurements were carried out using a Synergy™ 4 multimode microplate spectrophotometer (Biotek Instruments, Winooski, VT, USA) in ultrapure water. Fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared Triptolide-CQDs were recorded on the same instrument with excitation wavelengths ranging from 325 to 445 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed using a BRUKER TENSOR II FT-IR spectrometer (Billerica, MA, USA) in the spectral range of 500–4000 cm−1, employing KBr pellets as the substrate. For X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Triptolide-CQDs were deposited on a silicon wafer. XRD analysis was conducted using a D/MAX 2200 VPC X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Sendagaya, Tokyo, Japan) with Cu Kα1 radiation (λ = 1.54 Å, energy = 8.8 keV). XPS measurements were taken on an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (VG Scientific, East Grinstead, UK) using Al Kα radiation, with binding energies calibrated using the C 1s peak at 284.6 eV as the standard.

All SARS-CoV-2-Spike Vpp were purchased from the RNAi core of the Academia Sinica, Taiwan (http://rnai.genmed.sinica.edu.tw/). They were generated based on the vesicular stomatitis virus (VSV) packaging system with spike (S) protein of wild-type or different variants of SARS-CoV-2 and luciferase reporter gene. The Vpp infection assay was conducted essentially as previously described [24]. Briefly, 5000 cells were seeded in 96-well plates. The next day, cells were pretreated with different doses of compounds or DMSO (vehicle control) for 1 h. Then cells were inoculated with pseudovirions (MOI [multiplicity of infection] of 0.25 and 1 for 293T-ACE2 cells and all the other cell lines, respectively). After 24 h incubation, the CellTiter-Glo luminescent cell viability assay was performed to measure the cell viability. In parallel, each sample was mixed with an equal volume of luciferase substrate (Bright-Glo Luciferase Assay System, Promega, USA). The relative light unit (RLU) was measured immediately by the Synergy H1 Hybrid Multi-Mode Reader (Agilent BioTek, USA) and normalized with cell viability. Viability-normalized RLU was set as 100 % for the control group and the relative infection rates of the treated groups were calculated. All experiments were performed in triplicates and repeated at least three times independently.

For 293T-ACE2, Vero E6, H1650 and Calu-3 cells, the cytotoxicity of compounds was assessed by seeding 5000 cells in 96 well plates and cultured for 24 h, followed by treatment with different doses of compounds for another 24 h. For ID8 cells, 1000 cells were seeded in 96-well plates, followed by treatment with different doses of compounds for another 4 days. The cell viability was measured by the CellTiter-Glo luminescent cell viability assay system (Promega, USA). The RLU was measured immediately by the Synergy H1 Hybrid Multi-Mode Reader (Agilent BioTek, USA). The relative cell viability was calculated by normalization with the control group. All experiments were performed in triplicates and repeated at least three times independently.

For ID8 cells, 1000 cells were seeded in 24-well plates one day before treatments with different doses of compounds. Cells were fixed using 4 % paraformaldehyde after 6 days of treatment. Colonies were stained with 0.5 % crystal violet before plates were imaged, followed by quantification of colony number using Image J. Cell survival rate was calculated by normalizing the number of colonies in each well to that of the vehicle-treated well on the same culture plate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ELISA assay for spike-ACE2 protein interaction
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of triptolide on the interaction between SARS-CoV-2 spike protein and human ACE2 were measured by the SARS-CoV-2 ELISA Kit (AllBio). In brief, horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-conjugate-ACE2 was pre-incubated with different doses of triptolide (0.04, 0.2, and 1 nM) at room temperature for 30 min. Then the drug-treated ACE2 was added to the ELISA plate pre-coated with SARS-CoV-2 spike receptor-binding domain (RBD) at 37 °C for 1 h. Each well was then washed with washing buffer five times. After the last wash, 90 μl TMB (3,3,5,5-Tetramethylbenzidine, AllBio) substrate solution was added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 20 min. The color development was then stopped by adding a 50 μl Stop Solution, followed by the measurement of the HRP activity by detecting the optical density at 450 nm. The interaction between SARS-CoV-2 spike RBD and human ACE2 was calculated using the following equation: spike-ACE2 interaction (%) is the ratio of averaged OD450 values of the sample/DMSO control × 100 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biacore surface plasmon resonance (SPR) interaction analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The Biacore T200 system was utilized to investigate the interactions and determine the equilibrium dissociation constants (Kd) of the tested compounds. A CM5 Sensor Chip (Cytiva, GE Healthcare Life Sciences) was activated using a mixture of 0.4 M 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide hydrochloride and 0.1 M N-hydroxysuccinimide. Laboratory-produced recombinant wild-type SARS-CoV-2 S protein and human ACE2 protein were diluted to a final concentration of 50 μg/ml in 10 mM sodium acetate (pH 4.5) and covalently immobilized onto the chip surface, achieving immobilization levels of 6432.2 RU for the S protein and 5233.4 RU for the ACE2 protein. Triptolide was dissolved in 1 × PBS-P buffer (Cytiva, GE Healthcare Life Sciences) containing 5 % DMSO to a stock concentration of 50 μM and subsequently diluted to a series of concentration gradients in the running buffer (1 × PBS-P with 5 % DMSO). Similarly, Triptolide-CQDs-120 were prepared in 1 × PBS-P buffer to a stock concentration of 50 μM and further diluted into concentration gradients in the running buffer (1 × PBS-P). A low molecular weight (LMW) kinetics-affinity multi-cycle method was employed, with a sample injection time of 120 s at a flow rate of 30 μL/min, followed by a dissociation phase of 180 s. All experiments were conducted at 25 °C. Binding data were analyzed using Biacore T200 Evaluation Software (version 3.0).

Replicon assay was conducted essentially as previously described [37]. Briefly, 293T cells were transfected with 4 μg of SARS-CoV-2 reporter replicon (pBAC-SCoV-2rep) using LT1 transfection reagent. The expression of the replicon-driven GFP and luciferase was observed under a fluorescence microscope and measured using a luciferase assay kit, respectively.

Total cell lysates were prepared in RIPA lysis buffer supplemented with protease inhibitor cocktails, followed by separation by SDS-PAGE and transfer onto the PVDF membrane. Proteins were detected by incubation with primary antibody for overnight, followed by incubation with horseradish peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibody. The chemiluminescence signals were visualized by using the iBright Imaging system (Thermo Fisher, USA). Antibodies used in this study include ARID1A (#12354, Cell Signaling), MYC (#5605, Cell Signaling), GAPDH (#sc32233, Santa Cruz), and β-tubulin (#2128, Cell Signaling).

Total RNA was extracted using TRIzol reagent (Invitrogen) following the protocols recommended by the manufacturer. First-strand cDNA was generated by using the SuperScript® IV Reverse Transcriptase (Invitrogen) and oligo-dT primer. The 2−ΔΔCT method was utilized to calculate the relative expression of Myc by using Gapdh as an internal control. The following primers were used. Myc-forward: GCGACTCTGAAGAAGAGCAAG; Myc-reverse: GCCTCGGGATGAGTGAG. Gapdh-forward: CTGCGACTTCAACAGCAACT; Gapdh-reverse: TCTTGCTCAGTGTCCTTGCT.

All the animal studies were conducted in accordance with the Animal Use Protocol approved by China Medical University. Luciferase-expressing ID8-Arid1a KO-1 cells (1 × 106) were resuspended in 5 μL of PBS and intra-bursally injected into ovary capsules of the mice. Female C57BL/6 mice (aged 6–8 weeks, National Laboratory Animal Center, Taipei, Taiwan) were randomly assigned to each experimental group. The drug treatment started one week post-inoculation and was administrated intraperitoneally daily (vehicle or Triptolide-CQDs-180, 0.8 mg/kg). Tumor growth was monitored by IVIS imaging system (PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA) once a week. Mice weight was measured every day and mice were sacrificed 5 weeks after tumor cell transplantation.

All experiments were done at least three times. Data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (S.D.) from at least three independent experiments. Statistical analysis was performed with GraphPad Prism (GraphPad Software, Inc., San Diego, CA) using a one-way ANOVA test or the paired t-test, as appropriate. A P value less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Statistical significance was indicated as follows: P < 0.05 (∗), P < 0.01 (∗∗), and P < 0.001 (∗∗∗).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triptolide is a potential inhibitor of SARS-CoV-2 viral entry
ce:section.ce:para: Given the potent antitumor and antiviral activities of Chinese medicinal herbs, we set out to screen a library containing 225 natural compounds from Chinese Pharmacopoeia for the inhibitory effects on SARS-CoV-2 viral entry. To this end, we employed VSV particles pseudotyped with SARS-CoV-2 spike proteins, a SARS-CoV-2 Vpp infection assay (Fig. 1 A) we have utilized in our previous studies [24]. These pseudotyped particles not only adequately recapitulated the key features of SARS-CoV-2 entry into target cells, but also mimicked the critical determinants of cellular choice of receptors and proteases involved in SARS-CoV-2 entry [38,39].

We first employed SARS-CoV-2 Vpp carrying the spike from SARS-CoV-2 B.1, which is identical to the S protein of the original Wuhan-Hu-1 isolate, except for the presence of D614G mutation, hereafter referred as wild-type spike. 293T cells were engineered to express high level of ACE2 (293T-ACE2) and these cells were highly susceptible to entry driven by SARS-CoV-2 wild-type spike Vpp [24]. Among identified candidates of Chinese medicinal herbs that inhibited entry of SARS-CoV-2 Vpp, triptolide exhibited the most significant inhibitory effect (Fig. 1B). Next, we assessed the inhibitory effect of triptolide in a range of cell lines commonly used for in vitro studies of the SARS-CoV-like viruses for the analysis of cell tropism. H1650 and Calu-3 cells, derived from human lung adenocarcinoma, express both ACE2 and TMPRSS2, the critical entry receptor and entry activator for SARS-CoV-2, respectively [40]. The parental 293T cells, human kidney-derived epithelial cells, strongly express TMPRSS2, but lack ACE2 expression whereas 293T-ACE2 cells are highly permissive to SARS-CoV-2 entry. Compared to H1650 and Calu-3, African green monkey kidney epithelial cell line Vero E6 cells have comparable levels of ACE2 [41]. Importantly, SARS-CoV-2 viral entry into all these cell lines was highly dependent on ACE2 [42]. We found that triptolide inhibited wild-type Spike Vpp entry in a dose-dependent manner in all the cell lines tested at nanomolar concentrations (Fig. 1C−1F). Notably, we observed dramatic difference in the cytotoxicity of triptolide in 293T-ACE2 cells versus all the other cell lines, such as H1650, Calu-3 and Vero E6 cells (Fig. 1C−1F). This may be due to the broad potent antiproliferative activity of triptolide in almost all cancer cell lines tested so far [1,2]. Next, we measured 50 % effective concentration (EC50) and 50 % cytotoxic concentration (CC50) values in these cell lines and the selectivity index (SI) was determined by CC50/EC50 ratio (Fig. 1G). As compared to the known anti-SARS-CoV-2 compounds, Remdesivir and Chloroquine with SI of 130 and 89, respectively [43], triptolide exhibited a SI of 43, 27 and 21 in H1650, Calu-3 and Vero E6 cells, respectively (Fig. 1G). Consistent with a previous report showing much less cytotoxicity of triptolide on normal cells [44], we observed a much higher CC50 of triptolide in 293-ACE2 cells compared to lung cancer cell lines, such as H1650 and Calu-3, which may explain the dramatic difference between a SI of 4167 in 293T-ACE2 cells and those in other cell lines (Fig. 1G). In sum, these results suggest that triptolide may be a potential inhibitor for SARS-CoV-2 viral entry regardless of cell tropism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triptolide blocked Vpp infection of emerging SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variants
ce:section.ce:para: The Omicron variants, noted for their improved immune evasion and transmissibility, have quickly surged to global dominance since 2021 [45]. Several genetically distinct Omicron subvariants have appeared and overtaken each other. The Omicron BA.1 lineage, differing from Delta by nearly 30 amino acid substitutions in the spike protein, appeared in late 2021 and was replaced by lineage BA.2, which differed from BA.1 at 15 amino acid positions of spike. BA.4 and BA.5 lineages, more genetically similar than all other Omicron sublineages, likely emerging from within the BA.2 clade, became dominant in 2022. A recombinant of BA.2.10.1 and BA.2.275, XBB, emerged in late 2022 and gave rise to a new wave of infections [46]. Accumulating evidence has shown that the mutations in the Omicron spike protein may alter its cell tropism, interaction with ACE2, ability to enter cells, and evasion of therapeutic neutralizing antibodies [47−48]. Intriguingly, the spike protein of Omicron variants harbored a higher affinity for ACE2 compared with the original strain, as well as previous variants, such as Delta. Furthermore, the Omicron variants showed an enhanced entry efficiency via the cathepsin pathway, but a reduced efficiency via the TMPRSS2 pathway compared with the original or other strains [47−49]. These results have prompted us to examine whether triptolide can block the cell entry of Omicron pseudotyped virus. We first used 293T-ACE2 cells and found that triptolide efficiently blocked the Vpp entry of previous VOCs, including Alpha, Beta, Delta, Gamma, and Omicron (B.1.1.529) variants, as compared with the wild-type spike (Fig. 2 A). Furthermore, we employed Vpp of 5 Omicron descendent lineages that have become dominant throughout the world, including BA.1, BA.2, BA.2.12.1, BA.4/BA.5, and BA.4.6. Similar to wild-type spike, we observed an efficient inhibitory activity of triptolide at nanomolar concentrations on the Vpp entry of these emerging Omicron variants (Fig. 2B). Importantly, we further confirmed the high efficacy of triptolide in the inhibition of Vpp entry of Omicron variants in H1650 cells (Fig. 2C). Together, these results provide strong evidence to support the inhibitory effects of triptolide on cell entry of major SARS-CoV-2 strains, regardless of its mutations of spike.

It is well known that the clinical applications of triptolide are hampered by its high toxicity and low solubility in water [1]. To address these limitations, we set out to synthesize triptolide-derived carbonized materials in order to enhance its water solubility and reduce its toxicity. To this end, we have recently utilized a simple one-step dry heating approach to synthesize mannose-modified CQDs from solid ammonium citrate and mannose [35]. Additionally, using a similar method, we have synthesized a series of CQDs from curcumin that preserved the moieties of polymeric curcumin, significantly enhancing the antiviral activity of curcumin against enterovirus [36]. We therefore hypothesized that transforming triptolide into CQDs may enhance its antiviral activity. To test this hypothesis, we applied our recently established one-step dry heating protocol to synthesize Triptolide-CQDs by directly pyrolyzing triptolide in the presence of mannose with a mass ratio of 1:1 at 120, 150, or 180 °C for 2 h to prepare Triptolide-CQDs (Fig. 3 A). Heating the mixture of mannose and triptolide at temperatures ranging from 120 °C to 180 °C for 2 h produces dark brown residues. These residues are then dispersed in deionized (DI) water (5 mL) and sonicated for 1 h to form well-suspended CQDs solutions with a solubility >50 mg/mL, which is much higher than that of triptolide (<0.02 mg/mL). The formation mechanism of these CQDs involves several key processes, including dehydration, polymerization, condensation, and mild carbonization. The mannose molecules tend to pyrolyze compared to triptolide. Initially, the dehydration-induced condensation of mannose molecules leads to the creation of microscale supramolecular structures through polymer cross-linking. With continued heating, these structures undergo fragmentation and further carbonization to form carbon cores [36]. The triptolide molecules are in situ modified on the cores, ultimately forming spherical Triptolide-CQDs.

The synthesized CQDs, designated as Triptolide-CQDs-120, Triptolide-CQDs-150, and Triptolide-CQDs-180, correspond to the specific preparation temperatures of 120, 150, and 180 °C, respectively. Remarkably, the yield for all Triptolide-CQDs variants was observed to exceed 85 %, indicating a highly efficient synthesis process. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis reveals that all Triptolide-CQDs possess a consistent size distribution, with an average diameter of approximately 4.5 ± 2.3 nm (Fig. 3B). High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images provide further insight (insets to Fig. 3B), showcasing highly crystalline sp2-hybridized carbon cores with a 0.34 nm interlayer spacing, characteristic of the graphitic carbon (002) plane [50]. In Fig. 3C, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Triptolide-CQDs exhibit distinct broad bands at 20.6° and 40.7°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of graphitic carbon, respectively [51]. This confirms the presence of graphitic domains in the core of Triptolide-CQDs, further validating their crystalline nature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis provides detailed information about the surface chemistry of Triptolide-CQDs (Fig. 3D). The XPS survey spectrum shows prominent peaks corresponding to carbon (C1s), with binding energies centered around 284.8 eV. High-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s region reveal peaks attributed to C-C, C=C, C-O, and C=O bonds, indicating the presence of various oxygen-containing functional groups on the CQD surfaces and the formation of ester bond to link triptolide onto CQDs. These results further revealed the presence of phenolic and carboxylic groups, which contribute to the enhanced solubility and potential bioactivity of the CQDs. The UV–Vis spectra of Triptolide-CQDs display a distinctive peak at 275 nm and a broad shoulder extending from 310 to 375 nm. These features are attributed to π→π∗ and n→π∗ transitions, respectively (Fig. 3E), highlighting the presence of enriched oxygen-containing groups within the CQDs [52]. Under 365 nm excitation, Triptolide-CQDs emit a broad fluorescence spectrum, ranging from 400 nm to 700 nm, with a quantum yield of around 3−5 % (Fig. 3F). This broad emission is due to the presence of various-sized polycyclic aromatic or graphene clusters and surface defects [53]. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy of Triptolide-CQDs reveals vibrational signatures that are similar to those of triptolide, indicating that major moieties of triptolide are preserved on the CQDs (Fig. 3G).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triptolide-CQDs demonstrated superior antiviral capabilities against SARS-CoV-2 pseudo-particles
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we evaluated the antiviral capabilities of Triptolide-CQDs against SARS-CoV-2. Initial experiments revealed that carbonized mannose nanoparticles (Mannose-carbon nanoparticles, Mannose-CNPs), prepared exclusively from mannose at 120 °C, had no significant effect on viral entry, as indicated in Fig. 4 A. These findings underscore the essential role of triptolide molecules in enhancing the antiviral activity of CQDs. Subsequently, we investigated the antiviral efficacy of Triptolide-CQDs alongside original triptolide using wild-type spike Vpp in 293T-ACE2 cells. Of note, carbonized triptolide nanoparticles without mannose (Triptolide-carbon nanoparticles, Triptolide-CNPs), prepared exclusively from triptolide, as shown in Fig. 4B, exhibited no superior antiviral activity, with an EC50 of 1.7 nM compared to that of 0.96 nM for the original triptolide (Fig. 1C). Intriguingly, the antiviral effectiveness of Triptolide-CQDs was distinctly temperature-dependent. Among the variants tested, Triptolide-CQDs synthesized at 120 °C (Triptolide-CQDs-120) showcased the highest antiviral activity against wild-type spike Vpp. The EC50 values for Triptolide-CQDs-120, Triptolide-CQDs-150, and Triptolide-CQDs-180 were 0.08 nM, 11.9 nM, and 21.4 nM, respectively (Fig. 4C). Collectively, these findings underscore that converting the mixture of mannose and triptolide into CQDs significantly boosts its antiviral properties. Triptolide-CQDs-120, in particular, demonstrates superior antiviral activity, primarily due to the lower pyrolysis temperature which preserves more intact triptolide molecules on the CQDs. These densely packed triptolide molecules on the CQD surfaces may have a strong affinity for the viral particles, thereby enhancing antiviral efficacy.

To further characterize the cytotoxicity of Triptolide-CQDs, we initially assessed the cytotoxicity of carbonized mannose nanoparticles (Mannose-CNPs) and found minimal toxicity in 293T-ACE2 cells up to concentrations of 50 μM (Fig. 4D). Subsequent analyses of Triptolide-CQDs revealed a significant reduction in cytotoxicity compared to triptolide, with CC50 values increasing from 4 μM for triptolide to 14.1 μM for Triptolide-CQDs (Fig. 4E). Together, these results suggest that the incorporation of triptolide onto CQDs may not only enhance its antiviral efficacy but also mitigate its cytotoxicity.

Minnelide, a water-soluble analog of triptolide, has demonstrated potent anticancer properties in various preclinical cancer models and is currently under clinical evaluation for treating advanced solid tumors and leukemia [5]. A recent preprint study suggested that Minnelide may exhibit effects against SARS-CoV-2 in a murine model [54], though its ability to block viral entry in host cells remains unconfirmed. In comparative assays of triptolide, Minnelide, and Triptolide-CQDs-120 against the Vpp entry of the Omicron variant B.1.1.529, although Minnelide showed similar antiviral activity compared to triptolide, Triptolide-CQDs-120 demonstrated superior inhibitory activity over triptolide and Minnelide (Fig. 4G). Notably, Triptolide-CQDs-120 exhibited reduced cytotoxicity at concentrations as high as 5 μM, significantly outperforming both triptolide and Minnelide in terms of safety profile (Fig. 4G). Importantly, the enhanced performance of Triptolide-CQDs-120 was further confirmed in another cell line, human lung cancer cell line H1650 (Fig. 4H), underscoring the antiviral benefits of triptolide transformed into CQDs through mild carbonization. Together, the lower cytotoxicity and improved antiviral activity of Triptolide-CQDs-120 compared to traditional formulations could make it a preferable option in clinical settings where both efficacy and patient tolerance are critical.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triptolide disrupts the interaction of SARS-CoV-2 spike protein and ACE2
ce:section.ce:para: The above results indicate a potent inhibitory activity of triptolide and its derivatives on the entry of pseudo-typed SARS-CoV-2 regardless of cell tropism. Previous studies have demonstrated two mechanisms of SARS-CoV-2 entry into target cells following spike-ACE2 engagement. Depending upon the availability of the protease TMPRSS2, a key entry activator for SARS-CoV-2, the virus-ACE2 complex is either internalized into endolysosome for cathepsin-mediated activation or subjected to TMPRSS2-mediated activation on the plasma membrane [49]. Our data demonstrated that triptolide can efficiently block Vpp entry of emerging Omicron variants, which have a higher affinity for ACE2 and a lower efficiency in using TMPRSS2 compared with the original strain and other variants [47–49]. To directly test whether triptolide affects the interaction of spike-ACE2 interaction, we performed ELISA assay to measure the effects of triptolide on the interaction of SARS-CoV-2 spike and ACE2. As shown in Fig. 5 A, we found that triptolide blocked the spike-ACE2 interaction in a dose-dependent manner, with an IC50 of 267.3 ± 11.68 pM. These data suggest that triptolide may act to inhibit viral entry of SARS-CoV-2 through interruption of spike-ACE2 interaction. To test the engagement of spike and ACE2 proteins by triptolide and Triptolide-CQDs, we performed surface plasmon response (SPR) assay by using laboratory-produced recombinant wild-type SARS-CoV-2 spike and human ACE2 proteins. Our results demonstrated that triptolide binds to both ACE2 and spike proteins with a dissociation constant (Kd) of 4.33 μM and 135 μM, respectively (Fig. 5B). Notably, we observed a marked increase of binding affinity for Triptolide-CQDs-120 with both ACE2 and spike proteins as compared to triptolide, with a Kd of 0.88 μM and 0.91 μM for ACE2 and spike proteins, respectively (Fig. 5C). These results strongly suggest that triptolide and especially its derivatives, Triptolide-CQDs may engage both ACE2 and spike proteins to disrupt their interactions during viral entry.

Given the multivalent interactions between nanomaterials and viruses, we asked whether Triptolide-CQDs may also interfere with SARS-CoV-2 virus replication. To this end, we employed a replicon assay and measured the relative luciferase activity after transfection of a SARS-CoV-2 replicon with the luciferase reporter [37] (Fig. 5D). The positive control, remdesivir (RDV) markedly suppressed SARS-CoV-2 replication by 60 % at 1 μM. However, we did not observe significant inhibitory effects on replication upon treatment with Triptolide-CQDs-120 up to 100 nM. Taken together, these results suggest that the anti-SARS-CoV-2 activity of triptolide and its derivatives may be mainly through the interruption of spike-ACE2 interaction during viral entry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ARID1A deficiency predicts sensitivity to triptolide in ovarian cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: The prominent anti-SARS-CoV-2 capabilities and low toxicity of Triptolide-CQDs shown in Fig. 4 prompted us to investigate the anticancer activity of triptolide and triptolide-derived carbonized materials. In addition to high toxicity of triptolide, lack of biomarker to predict sensitivity to triptolide is also a concern for its anti-tumor application [1,2]. To address this knowledge gap, by using a bioinformatic approach, we found the sensitivity of ARID1A-deficient ovarian cancer cells to triptolide as described below.

A recent bioRxiv preprint demonstrated that small cell carcinoma of the ovary-hypercalcemic type (SCCOHT), a rare form (3–5 %) of ovarian cancer characterized by inactivating mutations in SMARCA4, the ATPase of the SWItch/Sucrose Non-Fermentable (SWI/SNF) chromatin remodeling complex [55], can be effectively targeted through triptolide [56]. As ARID1A is the most frequently mutated component of SWI/SNF complex in ovarian cancer (30–50 %), we set out to investigate whether ARID1A-mutated ovarian cancer cells predict sensitivity to triptolide. Specifically, alterations of SWI/SNF complex vary in different histological subtypes of ovarian cancer, with ARID1A and SMARCA4 mutations occurring in ovarian cancers that are often resistant to standard therapies [56,57]. Currently, there are no drugs directly targeting ARID1A mutations. However, the loss of ARID1A function may create unique vulnerabilities that may be exploited therapeutically [29]. Due to the limited clinical options available to patients with resistant ovarian cancers, we used a bioinformatic mining strategy to identify drugs to which ovarian cancer cells depleted of ARID1A are more likely to respond compared to cells with wild type ARID1A.

To this end, we integrated established PRISM (Profiling Relative Inhibition Simultaneously in Mixtures) v2 database, a repository containing drug response data, and the Dependence Map (DepMap) dataset of cancer vulnerabilities and dependencies. Through mining these datasets to analyze the correlations of ovarian cancer cell proliferation under drug treatment (from PRISM) vs ARID1A downregulation via RNAi or CRISPR/Cas9 (from DepMap), we focused on drugs which have statistically valid negative correlations (Fig. 6 A). This negative correlation suggests that these drugs may be selectively active in ARID1A-deficient cells whose survival may lose dependence on ARID1A and therefore ARID1A deficiency predicts its response to these drugs. Intriguingly, triptolide was among the top hits, along with multiple drugs that may share targets with triptolide, including Asaraldehyde, a natural COX-2 inhibitor [58,59] and Triamcinolone, a glucocorticoid receptor agonist [60]. Notably, among the top hits, there are a number of clinically relevant drugs that have been recently shown to have selective activity against ARID1A-deficient cells, including talazoparib, a poly ADP ribose polymerase (PARP) inhibitor approved for treatment of ovarian cancer, breast cancer, prostate cancer and pancreatic cancer [61], Volasertib, a polo-like kinase 1 (PLK1) inhibitor currently in Phase 2 study [62], and NVP-AUY922, a clinically actionable HSP90 inhibitor [63]. Together, these results suggest that this unbiased approach may be used to identify therapeutic liabilities engendered by ARID1A deficiency that could be exploited clinically.

Furthermore, we observed a significant negative correlation between cell survival upon triptolide treatment (triptolide survival) vs cell survival upon ARID1A depletion (ARID1A fitness) in human ovarian cancer cells (Fig. 6B). Consistent with the notion that ARID1A deficiency predicts sensitivity to triptolide observed in Fig. 6A, cell lines which are not dependent upon ARID1A for survival, i.e. those harboring deleterious mutations of ARID1A, exhibit higher sensitivity to triptolide (Fig. 6B). Together, these results suggest that ARID1A-deficient ovarian cancer cells could respond to triptolide.

To evaluate whether ARID1A loss may sensitize ovarian cancer cells to triptolide, we knocked out murine Arid1a using CRISPR/Cas9 in mouse ovarian tumor cell line ID8. We first confirmed successful deletion of Arid1a by immunoblot analysis, where a reduction of ARID1A levels was readily appreciated across two independent KO clones (Fig. 6C). No significant differences in cellular proliferation rate were observed comparing parental cells with isogenic Arid1a-null cells (data not shown). In contrast, when treated with triptolide, a robust increase in sensitivity to triptolide was observed in both Arid1a KO clones (Fig. 6D). Furthermore, a consistent and significant reduction in clonogenicity was also readily detected in both of the Arid1a KO clones after triptolide treatment as compared with parental cells (Fig. 6E). Thus, our results strongly suggest that ARID1A loss can increase the sensitivity of ovarian cancer cells to triptolide by affecting cell growth and survival.

Triptolide may interact with its targets either reversibly or irreversibly. Triptolide irreversibly binds to the XPB subunit of the general transcription factor TFIIH, thus blocking transcription initiation [64]. However, triptolide can also exert its effects in a reversible way. For example, triptolide induces distinct nuclear substructural changes in the nucleolus and nuclear speckles, which is reversible and dependent on both time and concentration [65]. Furthermore, triptolide also functions as a reversible inhibitor of the heat shock response [66] and dCTP Pyro-Phosphatase 1 (DCTPP1) [67]. To directly test the reversibility mode of triptolide in our system, we compared the viability of cells treated with triptolide either continuously or removed after different time points of treatment, including 12, 18, 24 and 30 h. We found that once the cells had been treated for 24 h, the anti-growth activity of triptolide became irreversible (Fig. 6F). Notably, this pattern was also observed in ARID1A-depleted cells (Fig. 6G). Together, these results suggest that the anti-growth activity of triptolide may be through irreversible association with its targets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triptolide decreased MYC levels at both mRNA and posttranslational levels
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have shown that triptolide may act as an interactive agent for super enhancers, eliciting its antitumor activity through disruption of super-enhancers [68]. Moreover, as one of the most commonly super enhancer-associated oncogenes, MYC was shown to be the direct target gene of SWI/SNF complex [69]. Furthermore, a recent study found an upregulation of mitochondrial membrane potential and respiration in ARID1A-deficient ovarian cancer cells, in part through upregulation of MYC protein and its targets [70]. Taken together, these data suggest that MYC signaling pathway may be a potential target in ARID1A-deficient ovarian cancers likely through multiple mechanisms.

To better understand how triptolide inhibits the growth of ARID1A-deficent ovarian cancer cells, we exposed ovarian cancer cells to triptolide for a range of treatment times to determine the mechanism of MYC regulation by triptolide. Frist, we observed that MYC protein level started to decrease after 4 h of triptolide exposure and continued to decline thereafter in Arid1a KO cells, but the drop of MYC protein levels did not occur until 10 h of triptolide treatment in the control cells (Fig. 7 A and B). These results suggest that triptolide may down-regulate MYC levels more potently in ARID1A-deficient cells. Second, to determine whether the down-regulation of MYC by triptolide occurs at mRNA level, we further determined MYC RNA expression in triptolide-treated cells. We observed a significant decrease of MYC RNA expression after 4 h of exposure to triptolide in Arid1a-KO cells, but not in control cells (Fig. 7C). Finally, in addition to its role in reducing MYC mRNA, triptolide has been shown previously to reduce MYC protein stability [71–74]. To directly test the effect of triptolide on MYC protein stability, we performed cycloheximide (CHX) experiment. The results showed that triptolide reduced MYC half-life in Arid1a-KO cells as compared to control cells (Fig. 7D). Together, our data suggest that triptolide may both decrease MYC mRNA and reduce MYC protein stability, which may explain the increased efficacy of triptolide in ARID1A depleted cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triptolide-CQDs excelled in the anticancer properties against ARID1A-deficient ovarian cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: Since Triptolide-CQDs are more potent than triptolide for anti-SARS-CoV-2 activity with lower cytotoxicity, we therefore compared their anticancer activities. To this end, by using the parental ID8 cells carrying wild type ARID1A, we first observed that Mannose-CNPs, carbonized nanoparticles prepared exclusively from mannose, had no significant effect on the colony formation assay, as indicated in Fig. 8 A and B. Moreover, we compared the efficacy of Triptolide-CNPs, carbonized triptolide nanoparticles prepared exclusively from triptolide, to the original form of triptolide. As shown in Fig. 8A and B, Triptolide-CNPs showed no superior efficacy in ID8 cells compared with triptolide, underscoring the crucial role of mannose in the transformation of triptolide to Triptolide-CQDs, as well as any favorable anticancer activity of Triptolide-CQDs. We noted that Minnelide did not show any significant anticancer activity compared to triptolilde (Fig. 8A and B). In line with temperature-dependent efficacy of Triptolide-CQDs, Triptolide-CQDs-180, carbonized nanomaterials synthesized at 180 °C from the mixture of triptolide and mannose, exhibited the highest anticancer efficacy as compared to Triptolide-CQDs-120 and Triptolide-CQDs-150 (Fig. 8A and B). Consistently, we confirmed the higher potency of Triptolide-CQDs-180 compared with Triptolide-CQDs-120 and Triptolide-CQDs-150 in an independent assay, cell viability assay (Fig. 8C). Overall, these results suggest that transformation of triptolide into Triptolide-CQDs may boost its anticancer activities, even though the optimal temperature that preserves its anticancer activity may be different from that for antiviral activities.

Finally, we evaluated whether Triptolide-CQDs preserved similar selective activity against ARID1A-depleted ovarian cancer cells as shown above for triptolide. To test this idea, we compared the clonogenic potential of Arid1a-deleted and isogenic parental mouse ovarian cancer cell lines upon treatment with triptolide, Minnelide, and carbonized nanomaterials prepared from triptolide, mannose, or the mixture of triptolide and mannose (Fig. 8D). In line with our observations in the parental cells containing wild type ARID1A (Fig. 8A and B), in both Arid1a KO clones, Triptolide-CQDs-180 outperformed all the other Triptolide-CQDs, as well as Triptolide-CNPs and Mannose-CNPs (Fig. 8D and E). These results suggest that Triptolide-CQDs-180 preserved the optimal anticancer properties of triptolide during the process of carbonization. Moreover, compared with ARID1A wild-type cells, both Arid1a KO clones are more sensitive to triptolide and Triptolide-CQDs-180, as well as high doses (10 nM) of Minnelide and Triptolide-CQDs-150 (Fig. 8D and E). Together, these findings strongly support the role of ARID1A deficiency in sensitizing ovarian cancer cells specifically to triptolide and its derived carbonized nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triptolide-CQDs attenuated tumor growth in an orthotopic syngeneic ovarian cancer model
ce:section.ce:para: By utilizing an orthotopic syngeneic mouse model of ovarian cancer, we evaluated the in vivo efficacy of Triptolide-CQDs-180, which exhibited the highest potency among all the triptolide derivatives tested in cell culture assays. We transplanted luciferase-expressing ID8-Arid1a KO-1 cells through intrabursal injection into female C57BL/6 mice and monitored tumor growth through IVIS imaging (Fig. 9 A). We observed that mice treated with Triptolide-CQDs-180 had significantly reduced tumor growth compared with those that received saline control (Fig. 9B and C). Furthermore, analysis of body weight did not suggest increased toxicity in mice treated with Triptoide-CQDs-180 compared with vehicle control (Fig. 9D). Collectively, these data indicate the in vivo efficacy of Triptolide-CQDs in attenuating ARID1A-deficient ovarian cancer growth.

Triptolide, a bioactive compound isolated from a traditional Chinese medicinal herb, has been extensively studied as an anticancer and antiviral agent [1,2]. However, whether triptolide exhibits anti-SARS-CoV-2 activity remains not fully understood. Here, through an unbiased screen of a Chinese medicinal herb library, we found that triptolide possesses the most potent inhibitory activity against SARS-CoV-2 Vpp entry at nanomolar concentrations (Fig. 1). We further synthesized water-soluble, triptolide-derived carbonized nanomaterials and demonstrated superior anti-SARS-CoV-2 and anticancer properties of these derivatives (Figs. 4 and 7). Thus, our study not only expands the antiviral spectrum of triptolide, but also, more importantly, warrants future development of triptolide and its derivatives as potential anti-SARS-CoV-2 candidates for the current pandemic and future outbreaks, as well as anticancer agents.

The spike protein of the SARS-CoV-2 Omicron lineages is characterized by the presence of numerous mutations in the N-terminal domain and RBD, which may enhance cell entry and enable antibody evasion [45]. Recent structural analyses have demonstrated that the Omicron variants exhibited 2–6-fold higher affinity for ACE2 than the RBDs of the SARS-CoV-2 isolate Wuhan-Hu-1 and the Delta variant [47]. In the case of Omicron sublineages harboring mutations with lower ACE2 affinity, such as BA.2.12.1, some Omicron-specific RBD mutations may establish new ACE2 contacts, leading to compensation for RBD mutations that weaken spike-ACE2 interaction and thus overall robust ACE2 binding of the Omicron spike protein [47–49]. Further, the Omicron spike protein has acquired a unique cell tropism regarding target cell choice and entry efficiency. First, the Omicron spike protein requires substantially higher ACE2 levels for efficient membrane fusion than other variants [48]. Second, unlike the spike protein of the original strain and all the previous VOC, the Omicron spike protein fails to use plasma membrane protease TMPRSS2 for efficient spike priming, resulting in a shift in cellular tropism away from TMPRSS2-expressing cells [47–49]. In our study, we employed multiple cell assay systems expressing critical cell entry proteins, including ACE2 and TMPRSS2 in 293T-ACE2, H1650, Calu-3 and Vero E6 cells, as well as Vpps derived from the wild-type, previous VOCs and the emerging Omicron variants. These systems allow us to assess the antiviral activity of triptolide across different cell lines and variants, comparison of which enables revealing potential mechanisms of action of triptolide. Intriguingly, triptolide maintains high potency in multiple cell lines against divergent Omicron sublineages that are currently dominating the pandemic (Fig. 2). Additionally, we found that triptolide was capable of directly blocking spike-ACE2 interaction (Fig. 5). Together, our findings support the notion that triptolide may function to block spike-ACE2 interaction during SARS-CoV-2 cell entry. The downstream targets of triptolide in SARS-CoV-2 infection remain unknown. Recent studies have identified the host metalloprotease ADAM9, ADAM10 and ADAM17 as facilitators of SARS-CoV-2 cell entry, in particular, in cell types that are TMPRSS2 independent [75,76]. Of note, although Omicron entry is impaired in TMPRSS2-expressing cells [47–49] and triptolide robustly inhibited Omicron Vpp entry (Fig. 2), we cannot exclude the possibility that TMPRSS2-dependent pathway may also be involved in the antiviral activity of triptolide. Further investigations are needed to provide a better understanding of how triptolide affects SARS-CoV-2 cell entry. Given that ADAM10 sheds a range of cell surface proteins involved in multiple signaling pathways, ADAM10 has been shown to promote cancer initiation and progression in numerous cancers. As the α-secretase responsible for Notch activation, ADAM10 is required for intestinal tumorigenesis in APC mutant mouse models [77]. Furthermore, ADAM10 is required for the survival and growth of cancer stem-like cells in both solid tumors [78] and in leukemia [79]. Interestingly, ADAM10-mediated shedding has been recently identified as a critical mechanism to promote cancer metastasis through a multitude of different substrates, such as EphA5 in prostate cancer [80], N-cadherin in ovarian cancer [81], and CX3CL1 in hepatocellular carcinoma [82]. Given that ADAM10 was previously identified as a target of triptolide involved in triptolide-mediated apoptosis in cancer cells [83], it would be interesting to test if triptolide may attenuate ADAM10-mediated functions in cancer, in particular metastasis and progression.

Due to its efficacy against various diseases, including cancer and virus infections, triptolide has attracted increased interest in the development of numerous analogs and their delivery strategies, including nanomaterials [1,2]. By applying our recently established one step dry-heating method [35,36], we synthesized a series of Triptolide-CQDs at different temperatures. The comprehensive characterization of Triptolide-CQDs underscores their potential applications in various biomedical fields (Fig. 3). Notably, the presence of enriched oxygen-containing groups and the enhanced solubility suggest that Triptolide-CQDs could play a significant role in antiviral and anticancer therapies. The dense moieties of triptolide on the CQD surfaces may interact with viral particles or influence the host's immune response, providing a novel approach to antiviral treatment. Furthermore, the unique structures of Triptolide-CQDs, particularly their highly crystalline sp2-hybridized carbon cores and condensed triptolide surfaces, contribute to their remarkable stability and bioactivity. These structural features not only enhance their interaction with biological molecules but also improve their electronic properties, making them suitable for various biomedical applications. The integration of specific oxygen-containing functional groups on the CQD surfaces further enhances their solubility and dispersion in aqueous media, which is crucial for their potential use in therapeutic settings. Overall, the unique structural attributes of Triptolide-CQDs make them a promising candidate for future biomedical research and applications.

Our data indicate that the antiviral activity of Triptolide-CQDs is temperature-dependent, with Triptolide-CQDs-120 showing a 10-fold lower EC50 whereas Triptolide-CQDs-150 and Triptolide-CQDs-180 showing 12- and 22-fold higher EC50, respectively, compared to triptolide (Fig. 4). The underlying mechanisms of temperature-dependent antiviral properties need to be further revealed in the future. In the case of curcumin, heating may induce a series of structural changes in curcumin through dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization to form core-shell CQDs whose surfaces may remain a pyrolytic polymer to enhance its antiviral activity [36]. However, curcumin-CQDs exhibited higher antiviral efficacy when synthesized at 180 °C than at 120, 150, or 210 °C [36]. This may be due to differences in the chemical and physical properties between these two compounds. Additionally, the multivalent interactions between carbon nanomaterials and viruses may also account for multiple mechanisms potentially involved in the antiviral capabilities of carbon nanomaterials [78]. Further studies are warranted to characterize the structural properties of these CQDs and their relationship with the antiviral potency.

Triptolide-CQDs represent a significant advancement in the development of antiviral agents against SARS-CoV-2 when compared to previously reported CQDs [84–87]. Unlike traditional CQDs, which primarily rely on the inherent antiviral properties of sole precursor, Triptolide-CQDs uniquely leverage the synergistic effect of mannose-derived core structures combined with densely packed triptolide molecules on their surfaces. The mannose core not only facilitates stable CQD formation but also promotes the preservation of high-density triptolide on the CQD surfaces, enhancing their specificity and affinity for viral particles. This targeted mechanism is particularly effective against SARS-CoV-2, as the triptolide molecules, known for their potent antiviral activities, are maintained in an optimal configuration to interact with and disrupt the virus. Furthermore, the mannose core of Triptolide-CQDs could potentially offer additional receptor-mediated antiviral mechanisms by interfering with viral entry pathways. In support of this idea, compared to triptolide, Triptolide-CQDs-120 exhibited 148-fold and 5-fold increases of binding affinity for SARS-CoV-2 spike and human ACE2 proteins, respectively (Fig. 5). These features collectively position Triptolide-CQDs as a superior antiviral platform, offering enhanced efficacy and specificity in neutralizing SARS-CoV-2 compared to other CQDs currently under investigation [85–88].

Despite its potent antitumor activity, the therapeutic application of triptolide is limited due to its toxicity, poor water solubility, and poor bioavailability. A number of triptolide derivatives with reduced toxicity and improved water solubility have been developed and evaluated in various preclinical cancer models and clinical trials [1,2]. Minnelide, currently in Phase 2 clinical trials for patients with advanced solid pancreatic cancer, is the most well-tested clinically for cancer treatment among all the triptolide analogs [4,5]. The potential anti-SARS-CoV-2 effects of Minnelide was recently reported in a murine model through bioRxiv [54], but it remains unknown whether Minnelide is capable of blocking viral entry. To this end, we compared the inhibitory effects of triptolide, Minnelide, and Triptolide-CQDs-120 on the Vpp entry of Omicron variants (Fig. 4G−H). We observed higher antiviral potency of Triptolide-CQDs-120, as compared to Minnelide or triotplide, which showed similar antiviral potency. Intriguingly, we found lower cytotoxicity in Triptolide-CQDs-120 as compared with Minnelide or triptolide. These results suggest that structural modifications in Triptolide-CQDs through carbonization may reduce the cytotoxicity of triptolide while retaining their antiviral potency.

Although the potent antiproliferative effects of triptolide have been documented in a broad range of malignancies, the biomarkers for the identification of selective tumors that are responsive to triptolide are still lacking [1,2]. Using a bioinformatic approach, we predicted the sensitivity of ARID1A-deficient ovarian cancer cells to triptolide. Consistent with this prediction, triptolide and Triptolide-CQDs-180 were both able to attenuate the growth of ARID1A-depleted cells at doses significantly lower than those required for cells carrying wild-type ARID1A (Fig. 6). Importantly, Triptolide-CQDs-180 were able to attenuate the growth of ARID1A-deficient ovarian tumors in an orthotopic syngeneic ovarian cancer model (Fig. 9). Thus, our study identifies ARID1A loss as a promising biomarker for ovarian cancer patients who may benefit treatment with triptolide derivatives.

ARID1A is considered to have context-dependent function in cancer initiation and progression. In liver cancer, ARID1A may promote tumor initiation whereas it may also suppress tumor progression. In mice with liver-specific Arid1a manipulation, loss of Arid1a presented resistance to tumor initiation while ARID1A overexpression accelerated initiation. However, in the established tumors, Arid1a loss promoted tumor progression and metastasis [89]. Consistently, the context-dependent function of ARID1A was also observed in primary versus metastatic endometrial cancers, where the primary tumors have wild-type ARID1A while the metastatic subclones carry deleterious mutations [90]. Another molecule, TGF-β has similar promotion vs suppression activity during different stages of tumor progression. Specifically, activation of TGF-β signaling impairs the appearance of primary mammary tumors in the earlier stages of tumorigenesis whereas TGF-β promotes the formation of lung metastases [91].

The relationship between ARID1A and ADAM10 has not been fully understood yet. But two sequencing studies in normal esophageal epithelium and esophageal adenocarcinoma indicated the co-existence of variants of both genes. In a patient with Barrett's esophagus-associated esophageal adenocarcinoma, co-existence of single-nucleotide variants of ARID1A and ADAM10 was identified [92]. Furthermore, in the esophageal epithelium of diethylnitrosamine-treated mice, deep sequencing identified eight mutant genes that were strongly positively selected, including Arid1a and Adam10 [93]. Further studies are warranted to dissect the functional relationship between ARID1A and ADAM10 in tumor development.

The wide expression pattern of ARID1A and ADAM10, as well as their involvement in multiple cell signaling pathways and other diseases suggest that their dysregulation may not be cancer specific. Consistently, tissue-specific knockout mouse models of murine Arid1a have been reported in different compartments, displaying context-dependent phenotypes [94–98]. In particular, human genetic studies have shown that mutations in ARID1A are associated with intellectual disability, autism spectrum disorder and Coffin-Siris syndrome [99]. Likewise, in addition to its role in cancer, increasing evidence suggests ADAM10 as a suitable candidate for therapeutic interventions in Alzheimer's disease through TREM2 shedding and generation of soluble TREM2 (sTREM2), known as a neuroprotective factor due to its enhancement of Aβ plaque degradation [100]. Importantly, a recent functional characterization of Alzheimer's disease genetic variants validated multiple physiological effects including reduced ADAM10 and altered sTREM2 shedding [101]. Together, to further examine the mechanisms by which triptolide functions with ARID1A and ADAM10 in conditions other than cancer will be novel avenues for future exploration.

Our results showed higher MYC levels in ARID1A-deficient ovarian cancer cells, and more importantly, triptolide reduced MYC levels by decreasing both MYC mRNA and MYC protein stability in ARID1A-depleted ovarian cancer cells (Fig. 7). The link between ARID1A deficiency and MYC expression we observed in ovarian cancer cells is consistent with previous reports in multiple cancer types. First, Nagl et al. showed that MYC was a direct target of SWI/SNF complex [69]. Second, Wang et al. demonstrated that ARID1A loss in human pancreatic ductal epithelial cells induced increased MYC expression and protein synthesis [102]. Third, Luo et al. showed in squamous cell carcinoma, ARID1A prevents tumor initiation and progression through inhibiting pRb/E2F1/c-Myc-mediated cancer stemness [103]. Finally, Xiao et al., showed in hepatocellular carcinoma, loss of ARID1A promoted cancer progression through up-regulation of MYC transcription [104]. Taken together, these results suggest that MYC signaling pathway may be a potential target in ARID1A-deficient cancers across different cancer types.

Our observations that triptolide both decreased MYC mRNA and reduced MYC protein stability in ARID1A-deficient ovarian cancer cells (Fig. 7) provide an explanation for the increased sensitivity of ARID1A-depleted ovarian cancer cells to this compound. In line with this anticancer mechanism of triptolide, previous studies in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma suggest that cells harboring the highest level of MYC are more sensitive to triptolide [105]. Furthermore, a recent report demonstrated enhanced response to triptolide in MYC-amplified medulloblastoma cells and that triptolide attenuated tumor growth through reducing both MYC mRNA and MYC protein stability [106]. As ARID1A mutations are prevalent in a wide variety of cancers, it will be interesting to further understand how triptolide and its derivatives act more potently in ARID1A-deficient cancer and to test the clinical efficacy of these drugs in tumors harboring defective ARID1A. Of note, loss of ARID1A has been shown to be a driver in the development of metastatic prostate cancer [107]. Furthermore, in breast cancer, ARID1A depletion increased E-cadherin/CDH1 expression and protein processing, leading to enhanced cell migration, invasion and metastasis [108]. Further research is needed to investigate the potential role of triptolide-ADAM10 axis in ARID1A-deficient cancer metastasis as it will provide a deeper understanding of the anticancer mechanisms of triptolide and identify new targets.

Collectively, our study reveals that triptolide inhibits cell entry of SARS-CoV-2 variants by blocking the interaction between viral spike and ACE2 receptor. Furthermore, water-soluble, triptolide-derived, carbonized nanomaterials exhibit superior anti-SARS-CoV-2 and anticancer properties with lower toxicity. Our results provide a rationale for the potential development of triptolide derivatives as promising anti-SARS-CoV-2 and anticancer agents.
